data.title: BILL Holdings: Eroding Underlying Demand Limits Growth Potential
data.description:
Summary

I am negative on Bill.com (NYSE:BILL). My summarized thesis is that underlying SMB demand is weakening, and this is now showing up clearly in BILL’s financials—slower customer adds and lower TPV per customer. While management continues to invest in product and embedded channels, the near-term financial impact looks minimal. With macro uncertainty rising and inflation risks growing from recent tariff policies, I believe BILL’s growth outlook is at risk of further downside.

Company overview

BILL is a financial operations software platform that mainly targets the small and midsize businesses (SMBs). Some of the key product highlights are automating back-office functions like accounts payable [AP], accounts receivable [AR], spend and expense management, and cross-border payments. As of 2Q25, BILL has ~481K total customers. It monetizes its services via subscription and transaction fees, with float revenue generated from interest on customer funds.

Macro Weakness Hits BILL Where It Hurts Most

The macroeconomic landscape has become increasingly challenging for SMBs, which is the main customer group for BILL, and I expect the situation to only get worse from here before it gets better. President Donald Trump's recent announcement of sweeping tariffs has introduced significant uncertainty into both the macro and business environments. These tariffs, including a 10% baseline reciprocal tariff on over 180 countries and a 145% tariff on Chinese goods, have led to retaliatory measures from trade partners. In my view, there are two major implications of imposing such tariffs, and both are very negative for BILL.

Firstly, this trade tension has a direct impact on consumer and business confidence. US consumer confidence plummeted to its lowest level in over four years in March, with households expressing heightened fears of a recession and inflation due to the tariffs. Similarly, small business confidence has also started to decline. This dynamic hits SMBs on two layers: (1) consumers spend less, which hurts the economy, and in particular, SMBs that have weaker balance sheets; and (2) SMBs start to shore up more cash for rainy days and not invest in the business (i.e., adopting the BILL platform).

Secondly, if we do not see a resolution to this tariff situation, my belief is that inflation will start to surge. Economists have noted that if the tariffs persist, inflation could rise to 4% by year-end, up from the current ~2.8%, and this could further erode consumer purchasing power (causing them to purchase less) and business operating costs (causing them to invest even less).

For BILL, whose product demand is directly tied to the financial health and transactional activity of SMBs, this macro backdrop is a very negative setup, in my opinion. The combination of increased SMBs operating costs, reduced consumer spending, and heightened uncertainty is likely to lead to decreased usage of BILL’s services, impacting its growth.

Customer adds and Engagement are slowing

And unfortunately, I think we’re already starting to see this macro weakness surface in BILL’s fundamentals. While total payment volume [TPV] still grew in 2Q25, by 13% y/y, a deeper look would reveal that customer adds are slowing, per-customer activity is weakening, and monetization is moving in the wrong direction. In my view, this is a sign that underlying demand is deteriorating.

For customer growth, while it is still positive, the pace has started to decelerate. BILL only added 4,500 AP/AR net new customers and 1,400 in Spend & Expense in 2Q25, bringing the total to 481,300. That implies only 2% y/y growth, a clear step down from previous years of >10% y/y growth. If you consider that BILL is still very under-penetrated in this industry (which means there are still a lot of whitespaces left), this slowdown only means that underlying demand is eroding.

Layered on top of declining customer adds is weaker platform engagement. In 2Q25, AR/AP TPV per customer (which I deemed as a more meaningful measure of wallet share) declined 1% y/y. This is an important point. 1Q25 AR/AP TPV per customer y/y growth was 3%. Given that AR/AP is BILL's core product, lower engagement here could be the first inning of lower engagement in other products. While management noted that they “continue to see very consistent numbers of transactions per customer,” the fact that TPV per customer is falling despite these steady transaction counts suggests that businesses are either transacting in smaller amounts or there is a change in spending behavior that reduces TPV flowing through BILL’s platform. Both are negative for BILL.

Embedded Channel Growth Is Not Delivering

The same dynamic—poor underlying SMB demand—can also be seen in BILL’s Embedded and Other Solutions segment. This business, which includes indirect distribution via partners like Xero and banks, was supposed to help BILL expand TAM and diversify revenue streams. In a way, this should’ve helped BILL accelerate growth, since it enables BILL to reach SMBs that might not have adopted the platform through its own marketing funnel. But that’s not what we’re seeing. In 2Q25, this segment's revenue only came in at $13.2 million, which was a 3% y/y decline. I do not doubt that BILL’s distribution reach has significantly expanded through its partners, but these numbers just reinforce my view that underlying SMB demand remains soft.

The hope is that new product innovation can reignite growth, but I don’t think this will happen. Take Advanced ACH, for instance. This product was highlighted as one of the key areas of the FY25 investment plan, with the goal of improving automation and reducing friction for large suppliers. But as of 2Q25, the product remains in beta, and management stated directly that it “won’t see material results from that in this fiscal year.” The same goes for invoice financing and some of the newer international payment products. I do believe these are interesting ideas with real potential, but none of them are going to move the needle in the near term. In other words, from a P&L perspective, this just means more cost (research & development) without the corresponding revenue, which means margin headwinds in the near term as well.

Valuation

Despite the visible headwinds, consensus is forecasting 13% y/y growth in FY25, which basically follows management guidance. My belief is that BILL is going to miss guidance and consensus estimates. Remember that the guidance was put out in early February, way before the tariffs were announced, which means the guide does not reflect the current SMB demand environment. BILL’s forward PE multiple is sensitive to its top-line growth, and we can observe this from how the market reacted when growth slowed to 14% in 2Q25 vs. 18% in 1Q25—the forward PE multiple collapsed from >40x to 30x instantly, followed by drifting down gradually to 20x today. If growth were too slow even further, which I think it will, BILL's valuation should see a lot more pressure. As a reference, ACI Worldwide (ACIW), which is expected to grow revenue at 9%, trades at 18x.

Investment Risk

If SMB spending rebounds faster than expected, leading to stronger TPV per customer and faster net adds, BILL growth could see strong acceleration. Additionally, if newer products ramp up earlier than guided (management may be conservative about guidance, hence, setting the stage for an upside surprise) and begin contributing meaningfully in 2H25, it could change the revenue trajectory. Embedded channel momentum could also surprise to the upside if a partner decides to allocate more resources to selling BILL’s product. Any one of these could reignite top-line growth and sentiment around BILL’s stock.

Conclusion

I am negative on BILL because I think the setup is getting worse, not better. Core metrics like customer add and engagement are trending down, and new initiatives won’t contribute meaningfully in FY25. More importantly, macro is getting more uncertain. With consensus still expecting double-digit growth, I think expectations need to reset lower—and when that happens, I see further downside risk to BILL’s multiple.

data.title: Apple: The China Fallout Has Begun
data.description:
Apple’s (NASDAQ:AAPL) stock is down YTD as the company’s growth prospects were already decreasing before the escalation of the global trade war earlier this month. The shares are now below the prices at which they were trading in late September when we published our previous article on Apple, in which we said that the company’s AI upside is overblown. Considering the rising challenges that Apple faces in China, rising macroeconomic risks, and questionable AI upside, we continue to believe that its market capitalization of around $3 trillion makes no sense and its shares remain a sell for us.

There’s No Reason To Be Excited About Apple’s Near-Term Future

While Apple managed to perform relatively well in the recent quarter, the amount of challenges that it now faces has increased dramatically and could undermine its growth opportunities later this year.

In our latest article on Apple, we argued that the company’s AI upside is overblown, and we continue to believe that that’s still the case to this day. Just last month, after Apple delayed the release of AI features to Siri, the company got sued in federal court for breaking the commitment to deliver the promised AI capabilities to its digital assistant. Without major features being released for now, there’s a possibility that we’ll see a slowdown in phone upgrades this quarter as customers might decide to stick with their original phones for the time being.

This is already happening in China where Apple’s sales are declining as there’s no urge for customers to buy new iPhones since Apple Intelligence is still not available to this day. The company’s CEO Tim Cook himself admitted that the lack of Apple Intelligence in China is an issue as iPhone sales declined in the country by 11.1% last quarter. Considering that at the same time, the smartphone market in China is growing, the lack of Apple Intelligence will likely continue to negatively affect the company’s sales in one of the biggest markets in the world.

However, even if Apple manages to release Apple Intelligence in China later this year with the help of a local partner, the worsening of Sino-American relations and the current trade war threaten to become a much bigger issue for the company. As of now, the United States charges a 145% tariff on Chinese-made goods. This is an issue for Apple since most of its iPhones are assembled in China and over 80% of its major suppliers manufacture their items in China as well. While on Friday it was announced that smartphones were exempt from the American tariffs, representatives of the Trump administration stated on Sunday that those exemptions are only temporary. As such, there’s a decent possibility that Apple’s costs will nevertheless rise over time as the company will likely become another casualty of the global trade war.

Considering that the tariff policy has also led to the decline of consumer confidence to one of the lowest levels in years, we don’t see a reason to be excited about Apple’s near-term future. While Apple’s stock has declined by around 20% since the start of the year, it still trades at multiples that are mostly greater than the sector median at a time when the underlying business received major downward revisions in the last few weeks. That is why we believe that Apple remains a sell in the current environment.

Risks to Our Bearish Thesis

If Apple manages to release the AI features for Siri later this year, then there’s a chance that there will be an uptick in phone upgrades, which can lead to higher sales that could offset some macro risks.

Also, Apple’s services business remains unstoppable at this point. Last quarter, Apple’s services business managed to exceed expectations and reached a new revenue record. With 2.35 billion active devices, Apple’s services business has plenty of opportunities to thrive for years to come and minimize the impact of macroeconomic and other risks in the future.

The Real Value of Apple

Our latest valuation model from September showed that Apple’s intrinsic value is $158.11 per share. Considering that Apple already faces some major headwinds that are unlikely to disappear anytime soon despite various growth opportunities, we believe that now is a good time to revise our model and make some changes to it given the latest developments.

In the new model below, we didn’t change the perpetual growth rate, which remains at 3% and closely aligns with the historical inflation rate and GDP growth rate. The model is created when Apple is trading at $198.15 per share, our forecast covers the period of the next five years, and the cash along with the total debt data have been taken from the recent financial report.

The discount rate in the valuation model is 8.47%, which we figured by calculating Apple’s cost of debt, the after-tax cost of debt, and the cost of equity. To calculate the cost of debt, we used the same interest expense data as in the previous model, while the total debt data was taken from the recent earnings report. For calculating the after-tax cost of debt, we primarily used Apple’s TTM data from the income statement. To calculate the cost of equity, we used a risk-free rate of 4.40%, a beta of 1.26, and a market return rate of 7.77%.

In the forecast table below, we assume a much slower sales growth rate than before because of the rising risks described earlier in this article. Wall Street also expects a single-digit growth rate for years to come, and recently greatly revised its estimates. We also don’t expect the growth of the EBIT margin as we did in the previous model since Apple will likely see a rise in costs due to the escalation of the trade war. We’ll likely decrease the margin expectations to even lower levels once the company reports its Q2 earnings results in a few weeks since we’ll be able to better understand how tariffs will affect its operations.

The tax rate stands at 16% in the model, which is close to what the management guided in the latest conference call. The assumptions for the remaining metrics are not that far away from Apple’s historical performance.

Those assumptions helped us figure out Apple’s enterprise value, which in our case is $1.82 trillion. At the same time, the company’s equity value is $1.78 trillion. That means that Apple’s intrinsic value is $117.41 per share, down around 40% from the market price. Therefore, we believe that Apple remains a sell for now.

Final Thoughts

Apple’s shares had a good run in 2024 mostly because of the announcement and later the release of Apple Intelligence. However, its near-term future doesn’t look promising. The continuous weakness in China along with rising macro risks could undermine Apple’s growth opportunities, while the questionable AI upside makes its stock a bad investment at the current price in our opinion. That is why we don’t think that there’s a significant upside to Apple’s overall business and its shares in the foreseeable future. Therefore, we still rate Apple as a sell.

data.title: FIDU: Industrials Dashboard For April
data.description:
This monthly article series reports subsector metrics, aiming at a top-down analysis of the industrials sector. It may also help analyze sector ETFs such as Industrial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLI) and Fidelity MSCI Industrials Index ETF (NYSEARCA:FIDU), whose largest holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italics describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each subsector: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for transportation in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in transportation companies. The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

data.title: El Pollo Loco Holdings: No More Poison Pills
data.description:
El Pollo Loco Holdings (NASDAQ:LOCO), based in California, is a fried chicken restaurant chain currently at the center of a special situation that could lead to the company's buyout and transition into private hands. Active investor Sardar Biglari, who operates Biglari Capital, has quietly accumulated 15% of the Loco stake and has already made an informal offer to acquire the remaining shares.

The Board has already signed a confidentiality agreement with Biglari Capital, and during the negotiation period, there is a limitation of stock accumulation. Although there are no official solutions yet, I can see a clear signal that this can be the first step towards a complete transition to private. Based on fundamental analysis and comparisons with other fast food companies, I believe that LOCO today offers an attractive price, with clear potential to rise to $12-14.

My valuation is BUY. I believe that investors have a great opportunity to take advantage of a market unnoticed company whose value can be realized both through the buyout and through the internal improvement of the activity in the coming years.

Biglari Capital

Biglari's takeover attempt: Active investor Sardar Biglari, who manages the investment company Biglari Capital. In April 2025, he signed a non-binding offer to acquire all shares in El Pollo Loco Holdings that he has not yet controlled. Biglari has already accumulated about 15.1% of the company's shares through various related structures, which makes it the highest shareholder (about 4.5 million shares).

An important step took place on April 6, when the Loco Board signed a confidentiality agreement with Biglari Capital, which includes a standstill condition - Biglari cannot acquire more shares during negotiations. This indicates a constructive tone between the parties and increases the likelihood that the offer will be taken seriously. Although the Board has not yet made the final decision, the fact that only one buyer is included in the negotiations shows that the transaction may be real.

What if a risk is the previous caution of the board. In August 2023, when Biglari began to actively accumulate the shares, the company activated the so-called "poison pill" to prevent control without the consent of the board. This threshold was later increased from 12.5% to 15%, but Biglari had already crossed the limit, reaching 15.1%. This means that further action can only be the way of negotiations, not aggression.

All of this shows that the board understands the value of the company and seeks to make any takeover in good faith and at the correct price, reflecting the long-term potential of the company.

If Biglari succeeded in implementing this transaction, El Pollo Loco would probably be removed from the stock exchange and become part of a private ownership portfolio. This would allow Biglari to implement long-term transformations without a quarter results pressure. His strategy has always been focused on taking control, optimizing performance, and creating value through restructuring.

Biglari has proven experience in the restaurant sector. In the past, he took over Steak 'n Shake and Western Sizzlin, which was transformed through strict cost control, structure optimization, and long-term strategic control. This success story shows that he knows how to realize value in inefficient restaurant chains.

In addition, this action may be a breakthrough point in Loco's history - if the company is acquired, it will serve as a reference point where the previously underestimated public company will be evaluated by its true value. This is especially important when Loco has a strong brand, activity margins still have space to grow, and experience and efficiency can be improved by Biglari control.

How much could El Pollo Loco be worth?

Chuy's Holdings

In 2024, Darden Restaurants Inc. (DRI) acquired the entire Chuy's Tex-Mex network, comprising 101 restaurants operating across 15 states.

Transaction value: ~605 million

Premium: 40%

EBITDA: 10.3x

Del Taco Restaurants, Inc.

In 2021, Jack in the Box Inc. (JACK) purchased Del Taco with ~ 600 restaurants in 16 states.

Transaction value: ~575 million.

Premium: 66%

EBITDA: 7.6x

Ruth's Hospitality Group Inc.

In 2023, Darden Restaurants Inc. (DRI) purchased a chain of luxury steak restaurants with 154 locations.

Transaction value: ~715 million.

Premium: 32%

EBITDA: 9.4x

J. Alexander's Holdings Inc.

In 2021, SPB Hospitality LLC acquired J. Alexander's chain with 47 locations.

Transaction value: ~220 million.

Premium: 78%

Fiesta Restaurant Group.

In 2023, Authentic Restaurant Brands purchased a Pollo Tropical network, operating 134 restaurants in Florida.

Transaction value: ~225 million.

Premium: Only 8%, the company was in a difficult financial position.

What does that mean by El Pollo Loco investors?

These examples show that a typical takeover transaction in the restaurant sector offers a 30% to 70% premium for investors, and usually, EBITDA ranges from 7 to 10x. If we apply a conservative scenario in the case of El Pollo Loco (8.5x EBITDA), we would receive about $13-14, which would mean more than 35-40% potential return from the current price of around $9.80.

Most importantly, it shows that Biglari's intentions are not speculative, as similar transactions are common and frequent in this sector. Given Biglari's capital size, restaurant management experience, and 15.1% of the ownership stake, this situation seems favorable.

Peer Comparison

The company is not only undervalued, but also is on strong growth in profitability, which the market is currently ignoring.

The EV/EBITDA ratio is just 9.65, while many other publicly listed restaurant chains are significantly higher, like Potbelly (PBPB), 15.38, First Watch (FWRG), 18.67, and Portillo's (PTLO), as much as 16.94. While some smaller chains have lower EV/EBITDA, the Loco is well below the sector average.

P/E is currently 11.38, which is close to the market average, but given that Loco EPS growth is +16.22% per year, which means that the shares are currently traded with an extremely low PEG (~ 0.70). It is one of the classic signals that the profitability of the company is not priced in the market properly.

Compared to competitors such as Denny's (DENN, EPS +17.1%, EV/EBITDA 8.78), BJ's Restaurants (BJRI, EPS -14.6%, EV/EBITDA 11.37), or Portillo's (PTLO), EPS +43.7%), LOCO has a balanced growth and moderate valuation of profitability. Other companies are either too expensive or face great challenges.

In addition, although Loco's revenue growth (+0.93% YoY) is not impressive, it is positive, along it maintained EPS growth, so the company is already implementing efficiency increases. This is another positive signal to a potential buyer as Biglari, that it can quickly increase EBITDA using the sector's experience. Investors can not only speculate on the result of M&A, but also to hold it as a value investment based on fundamentals

Risks

The biggest risk is that Biglari Capital and Loco Board will not agree on price or conditions. Historically, El Pollo Loco Board demonstrated aggressive defense (poison pill tactic in 2023), so there is no guarantee that the proposal will be accepted. If the transaction does not take place, investors can expect a return to a fundamental price of $8.50-9.00, which would mean ~ 10-15% correction from the current level.

Even if the negotiations were constructive, they can last for months. At that time, the share price can fluctuate without a clear direction. Investors seeking fast returns may be disappointed with long-lasting uncertainty.

Loco's revenue growth has been just +0.93% in recent years, and competition in the restaurant market is intensifying. If the performance results are going to be worse, it may reduce the attractiveness or reduce the price of the offer.

Although Biglari has experience in restructuring restaurant chains, his management style is often criticized as too centralized and confrontational. There is a risk that this reputation may result in the Board rejecting his proposal, even if it is financially attractive.

Conclusion

El Pollo Loco is currently in an attractive investment situation. With Biglari Capital, already holding 15.1% of shares and ongoing negotiations, there is a real opportunity to get a 30-40% premium from the current ~ $9.80 price. Even without a transaction, the company seems undervalued, with 16.2% EPS growth and conservative P/E and EV/EBITDA indicators.

Risk exists, but the expected reward is attractive. I believe that now is the time to enter the position. So I rate this stock as a BUY.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Stock With A Catalyst investment competition, which runs through April 17. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: China Mengniu: Bullish On Milk Price Recovery And Margin Expansion
data.description:
China Mengniu Dairy Company (OTCPK:CIADF) (OTCPK:CIADY) (2319.HK) or "CMDC" is still worthy of a Buy in my view.

My prior January 2, 2025 article assessed the company's multi-year financial prospects.

I remain a bull on CMDC. The stock will benefit from a rebound in raw milk prices, while its mid-term operating profitability outlook is favorable.

Price Of Key Commodity Is Likely To Have Bottomed

CIADY's topline contracted by -10 percent to RMB 89B in FY24. This was 2% below the sell-side consensus estimate as per S&P Capital IQ data. The company had done better with a +7% sales increase in the preceding year.

In its earnings release, CIADF explained that "supply imbalance" was the main reason for the disappointing performance. It was referring to a decrease in the price of raw milk, a critical input for dairy products.

CMDC's GPM ("Gross Profit Margin") would have been boosted by lower commodity-related expenses. But this is offset by other negatives.

Mengniu recently participated in an investor meeting hosted by Goldman Sachs (GS) on April 1, and mentioned that the Chinese dairy sector was in a "deconsolidating trend" last year. This management commentary is taken from GS's report ("unavailable in public") titled "NDR Takeaways" issued on the same day.

CIADF's less sizable rivals are able to survive and even thrive during periods of depressed raw milk pricing. The sourcing advantage that CMDC enjoys due to its scale is partially nullified. This environment also encourages stiffer competition in the form of aggressive discounting. It's easy to understand why the company's revenue declined in the prior year.

Also, CIADY's FY2024 bottom line also took a CNY 827MM hit, because of the losses from its associate "Modern Dairy" (1117.HK). The latter is an upstream dairy player that had asset write-downs brought about by price weakness. The core '24 net profit for CIADF decreased by -8% to RMB 4.4B.

Raw milk prices in China ("PRC") have dropped by almost 30% to slightly below RMB 3.0 per kilogram since the beginning of 2022. These numbers are obtained from a March 31, 2025 news report published on Chinese media portal Sina.

We should have already seen the bottom of the cycle.

At the GS event mentioned above, CMDC shared its observations that farms have begun to reduce "their cow herd size".

Securities firm "China Post" came up with a report titled "Dairy Farms Deep-Dive" on April 8, 2025 that isn't publicly available. Its research suggests that the PRC's number of dairy cows went down by -6% YoY to approximately 6.1 million in 2025 this far. This is getting closer to the 5.9-6.0M level which the researchers view as the demand-supply equilibrium point.

I forecast that CIADF will register positive revenue and normalized net income growth rates of 3% and 15%, respectively for FY25. This assumes a recovery in raw milk prices. In this scenario, CIADY gains market share due to sector consolation, and there's an absence of impairments for its associate company.

Company Can Realize Intermediate-Term Margin Target

My favorable opinion of a potential turnaround for Mengniu this year is also supported by its profitability enhancement efforts.

CMDC disclosed at its 2H24 briefing the goal of increasing its EBIT-to-revenue ratio by +0.9-1.5 ppts ("percentage points") in the FY25-27 time frame. I think this is doable. The transcript for this call is sourced from S&P Capital IQ.

One margin enhancement lever is expanding the revenue base for certain sub-scale divisions.

As an example, the GPM of CIADY's niche chilled yogurt business is more than -200 bps below the group-level metric. The company has lately rolled out new zero-sugar variants and additional flavors to drive up sales of this product category. When CIADF's yoghurts become popular among a bigger group of consumers, the margins will most probably rise due to fixed-cost leverage effects.

Another thing to watch is CMDC's venture into foreign regions, which represented a modest 5% of its FY2024 topline.

At the company's 2H2024 earnings call, it highlighted that SouthEast Asia ("SEA") has 700M people with household incomes a third of China. This means that this is a huge addressable market whose residents are at the early stage of becoming more affluent. It also noted that the ASEAN ("Association of Southeast Asian Nations") sector is less price-competitive than the domestic industry based on its checks. CIADF's OPM for the company as a whole will likely get better, if it secures a foothold in higher-margin overseas countries with solid potential like SEA.

Risk Factors You Would Consider

There are situations which would make Mengniu a less attractive proposition.

CMDC's topline performance might still be poor and losses at its associate could continue, in the event that the milk price doesn't recover.

It's possible that CIADY's future profitability is weaker-than-expected, because of a failure to execute on foreign expansion and new product initiatives.

My Closing Thoughts

My Buy rating for CIADF is left unchanged.

CMDC has a capital appreciation potential of +29% based on my target earnings multiple of 18 times. This is what the market currently values its peer Yili (600887.SH) according to S&P Capital IQ.

Mengniu is now trading at 14x consensus forward P/E, and the discount is unwarranted. The Chinese UHT ("Ultra-High Temperature") milk market shares for CIADY and 600887.SH are similar at 43% and 44%, respectively.

An improved set of results for CIADF this year should prompt a re-rating of the company's shares.

data.title: Webull: We See 60% Downside From Here
data.description:
A few weeks ago, conservative media company Newsmax (NMAX) listed on the public markets to much fanfare.

With an initial trading price of roughly $14 per share, the stock went on to rally more than 1,800% following a massive frenzy from investors. Briefly, the company was more valuable than Fox Corporation (FOX), another conservative media firm many consider to be NMAX's much, much larger rival.

Following this initial excitement, however, shares have languished, trading back down into the $20-per-share range. This represents a massive wipeout of more than 90% - from highs to lows - for investors, driving losses for almost anyone who bought into the spike:

To many market watchers, this cycle was somewhat predictable, given NMAX's breakeven profitability, small operating footprint, and ludicrous valuation.

Monday, shares of Webull (NASDAQ:BULL) rallied more than 370% in one of the hottest new issue debuts of the last few years - similar to NMAX (Although the stock has since come off somewhat).

The excitement here is being driven by the considerable interest that institutional investors have in Fintech companies, but also the firm's tightly held share structure. Sure, there are a lot of shares outstanding, but the founders and insiders hold a great deal of this float. Thus, the circulating share count may actually be smaller than it appears on paper, which could be behind the stock price's recent elasticity. Less shares means that smaller amounts of money can push the price around more quickly.

That may be what we're seeing here.

Now, at this price point, BULL is valued at roughly $17+ billion dollars. To us, this price is simply ridiculous, given that the company only did ~$390 million in revenue in each of the last three calendar years. This places the sales multiple at roughly 43x.

Over the long run, we see value in Webull. The company's operational footprint is robust, the product offering is solid, and we see the potential for material growth in top and bottom-line results in the coming years.

However, for a company that hasn't grown revenues as of late, this price point appears way out of whack. Thus, we're expecting a similar, NMAX-style price drop over the following year or so.

Today, we'll outline BULL's financial profile, talk about the stock's valuation, and explain why we see considerable downside in the company's shares.

Sound good? Let's dive in.

BULL's Valuation

Normally, we'd talk first about the company's financials, but there's a bit of a unique situation going on with Webull.

Given that the stock just completed a SPAC in the last few days, there's very little 'hard' financial information about the company out there right now. Even figuring out the basics like share count and valuation has required an extensive search.

As we've been able to piece together from regulatory filings and other financial data, the company has roughly ~485 million shares outstanding at the present moment.

In the company's recent F-4 filing, the below table outlines how many shares could potentially be outstanding following a business combination:

As you can see, most of the new listed company is owned by former BULL investors, although some of the SPAC investors are also in on the action.

Depending on how many shares were redeemed in the transaction (a number we haven't been able to find), this table outlines that there may be anywhere between 515 million and 490 million shares outstanding. This is in slight contrast with market cap data we're seeing on the company's website and in a recent CNBC article, which indicate an outstanding share base between 460 million shares and 470 million shares. All told, it's a bit of a mess when it comes to the data, but we'll be operating under the assumption that BULL has roughly ~485 million shares outstanding, which is essentially the midpoint from all of the information that's out there.

With a stock price on Wednesday of $35.37, this gives the company a market cap in the ~$17-billion-dollar range.

This makes BULL larger than peers like SoFi (SOFI), and in the same ballpark (tens of billions in market cap) as industry heavyweights like Robinhood (HOOD):

Company Market Cap Sales Multiple
Schwab $139 billion 7.2x
Interactive Brokers $73 billion 3.6x
Robinhood $39 billion 13x
Webull $17 billion 43x
SoFi $12 billion 4.3x

Unfortunately, there aren't that many great public comps for BULL, as other firms like EToro, Public, and TastyTrade remain privately held.

That said, we'd argue that the company's stock, at this price, appears expensive. The current market cap already positions the stock as the 6th largest brokerage company listed in the United States.

We'll talk more about this later.

Now that we have some idea of where BULL is currently positioned from a size and valuation perspective, let's take a look at some of the company's underlying financials.

BULL's Financials

As we mentioned, many of BULL's 'hard' financial performance metrics aren't available, as the firm hasn't yet released public, audited numbers in the form of a 10Q or 10K.

Right now, we're going off of BULL's investor presentation, which highlights a couple of things.

First off, on the product side, we're actually quite happy with BULL as an operator within the space.

Over the last few years, the company has built up a huge account base (more than 23 million users), alongside a robust suite of offerings.

Catering to both casual investors and more active traders, BULL offers access to a number of investment products and features across a relatively wide number of global markets:

Since 2022, the company has expanded regulatory licenses from 3 to 12, expanding the TAM and growth potential of the company. Not only that, but BULL's actual offering includes basically everything you'd ever want (aside from crypto), for both traders and investors:

When viewed in a more structured layout, we'd argue that the breadth and competitiveness (zero commissions) of the offering is robust, even when compared with other firms like HOOD:

Assets Accounts Tools Platforms

Stocks & ETFs

 Cash Recurring Investments Web
Options Margin Smart Advisor Desktop
Index Options IRA Fractional Trading Mobile
Futures Overnight Trading
Bonds Charting, Screeners, Research
Money Market Funds

That said, the company's financials don't necessarily reflect this reality.

Nominal revenues have been solid, but ongoing expansions have dampened recent profitability, highlighting the depth of recent investments, and how long it might take for those investments to pay off.

For revenues, BULL's top line has largely remained flat from 2022 through today:

While the company may highlight that this performance as 'self-hedging', and thus, a good thing, the lack of growth - in a period where other companies have grown substantially - is somewhat concerning.

Similarly, on the bottom line, tightening margins have characterized recent years' performance, as stronger investments into marketing and G&A have driven down adjusted operating income:

This dynamic is typically one you'd see during an expansion, so it isn't problematic in and of itself, but when paired with the static revenues, it's something to think harder about.

All in all, what little we have in the way of BULL's financial performance shows that the company has a clear product-market fit with respect to the client experience, although monetizing those customers has been a challenge.

Plus, the company's multi-country strategy means that the firm's 23.3 million+ client base could be worth significantly less in LTV than HOOD's almost fully American user cohort, by comparison.

Should You Buy Or Sell Webull?

So - this brings us to the million-dollar question. Should you buy Webull? Or sell it, given the generous stock price?

To answer that, we'll need to make a couple of assumptions. First off, we're anticipating that as brokerage headwinds dissipate and investments begin to pay an ROI, that BULL's top line results will begin growing once more.

Not only that, but we're also anticipating that margins will re-expand off the back of this growth. Neither of these dynamics may play out, but its' how we see the future going, given the offering quality. With product breadth and depth like BULL, there's always an opportunity for growth.

From there, we see BULL's revenues hitting the $500 million mark by the end of 2026. This is a rough prediction, but its' based, again, on the company getting ROI from the expanding global market presence.

Finally, on the valuation front, as we comped out earlier, BULL - in that growth case - could be worth anywhere between 6x and 10x sales. SOFI isn't a great comparison at the lower end of the range, but BULL's results are dwarfed by HOOD's stronger NA entrenchment and more deliberate foreign offerings. Thus, multiples between 13x and 4x.

At 6x - 10x the potential of FY'26 $500 million in sales, you're looking at a market cap of roughly $3 - $5 billion. This is a significant discount vs. where the market is currently trading, which is at a much higher ~$17 billion valuation. Thus, we see significant 60%+ downside from the current stock price, which results in our 'Strong Sell' rating.

For reference, this indicates a price in the $10 - $11 per share range.

Risks

On the 'risk' side of things, we've covered both the positive and negative aspects of the company.

However, when taking something like this short, it's worth considering that frenzies and rallies can last for several days or weeks, and that a 'short' at this price may be nowhere near the top.

Thus, we'd argue that the real way to manage risk here, if one was to take it 'short', would be by sizing appropriately.

Even if you're not looking to take it short, if you're currently long, then we'd advocate that you sell out at the current price. No need to be greedy with an already monstrous gain.

Summary

Taken together, here's our overarching view: the company has a solid offering, and the business is stable. Plus, there's opportunity for more growth in the coming quarters from the expanding TAM and feature set quality.

However, at this price, the market has baked in way too much growth and excitement. As a result, even with the potentially strong underlying performance, we're finding it hard to rate BULL as anything other than a 'Strong Sell' for the coming year.

Stay safe out there!

data.title: TSMC Has Finally Become Dirt Cheap Following Its Q1 Results (Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

I initiated my investment coverage of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (NYSE:TSM) here on Seeking Alpha in late February 2024 with a "Buy" rating, which hasn't aged well so far, given the recent volatility spike on the markets and rising concerns around the US-China trade war with all subsequent consequences.

My Updated Thesis

In my most recent article, which was a bullish update, I argued that TSMC was undervalued by over 22% given its marginality levels and also the news about OpenAI's partnership. Now, even when I account for the rising risks and uncertainty in valuation calculations, my valuation findings say that TSM stock has simply become too undervalued to ignore. The firm's recent news - specifically the rise in pricing for its chips - suggests that TSMC has an unbeatable moat in its niche with a solid pricing power, which should compensate well for most tariffs and possible growth limitations. I'm upgrading TSM stock to "Strong Buy" following its fantastic Q1 FY2025 print.

TSM's Q1 FY2025 Review

As I'm writing these lines, Taiwan Semiconductor managed to release its Q1 FY2025 results just a couple of hours ago, beating the consensus estimates, showing a remarkable consistency across its business units:

With consolidated revenue of ~$25.53 billion (+35.3% YoY) and diluted EPS of ~$2.12 (per ADR unit, +60% YoY), the slight sequential dip of 3.4% (5.1% in USD terms) compared to Q4 2024 looks much better across the main segments compared to what could be observed in 2024/2023. We see some powerful underlying demand drivers, particularly the continued ramp-up in AI-related applications, which partially offset the smartphone softness, based on the management's commentary.

But what impresses me most are the sustained, industry-leading margins: TSM's GP margin came in at a stellar 58.8%, while operating and net margins were at 48.5% and 43.1%, respectively. The firm's pricing power is evident, especially so in its most advanced nodes. TSMC isn't just an ordinary chip manufacturer; it is the indispensable foundry partner for nearly every major fabless semiconductor company globally, being the world's largest dedicated chip foundry, with ~65% market share, according to Statista. The global semiconductor foundry market should reach ~$321.1 billion by 2034 in TAM, according to Global Markets Insights (with a CAGR of >9%), and TSM's wide economic moat there is what its peers struggle to breach. So I believe the market's recent pessimism has excessively discounted the company's fundamental strengths and long-term growth trajectory, given the estimated CAGR of the end market.

Free cash flow in Q1 also saw a healthy increase, rising by $1.12 billion to $9.07 billion (my conversion here is based on today's FX, as is), even as the company invested heavily with CAPEX totaling ~$10.06 billion during the quarter. At the same time, the amount of cash on the balance sheet went up by 37.9% YoY in (NT$ currency), with shareholders' equity rising by 64.5% YoY; the ROE of 32.7% showed an YoY expansion of 730 bps, which is a lot.

Looking ahead, the management guided for Q2 FY2025 revenue to be between $28.4-29.2 billion, way above the consensus estimate of ~$27.22 billion prevailing before the announcement (based on SA News), which is a great sequential growth path (+12.8% QoQ at the midpoint):

TSMC also expects to maintain its exceptional profitability with a GP margin of 58% and an EBIT margin of 48% (both at the midpoint) despite the swirling tariff uncertainties.

So, assuming the management could overlook the world's development since Trump's inauguration, it's clear to me that they still see a strong demand for their cutting-edge 3nm and 5nm technologies, which power the most demanding applications in AI, high-performance computing, and premium smartphones.

The Recent Developments And Tariffs

Tariffs are now the elephant in the room for any semiconductor company, and the problem here is that the news flow surrounding this has been fast, furious, and, to be honest, a bit confusing. Initially, as you might remember, President Trump announced "reciprocal tariffs," including rates cited as 34% for China and 32% for Taiwan; and even as the fear of such tariffs disrupting supply chains, inflating costs for consumers and businesses, and potentially dampening demand for electronics manufactured using TSMC's chips arose, Trump added fuel to the fire stating that "he warned TSMC it would face a hefty tax (up to 100%) if it didn't build factories in the U.S.":

I can't know what exactly influenced Trump then, but the administration subsequently announced exemptions for a range of popular electronic goods - smartphones, laptops, computers, processors, and memory chips - primarily imported from China. This naturally gave a massive relief, causing a rebound in many tech stocks. But then again, the Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick soon characterized the exemptions as merely a "temporary reprieve," and the tech sector fell off a cliff.

While politicians "are trading" during this ongoing trade war, I think what's important amid all that are signals coming from tech companies, which were considered "wide-moaty". TSMC is exactly such a company, and even if we don't look at its just-released Q1 figures (those may not be relevant in the next few quarters), we can see from the recent announcements from the firm that its pricing power is still there:

According to a report by the Economic Daily News, market sources suggest that TSMC is considering raising prices for 4nm chip production at its U.S. facility by as much as 30%, citing robust demand and rising costs.

It looks like all the tariffs risk for TSMC has already been priced in, perhaps excessively so, given the exemptions (however temporary) and the sheer indispensability of TSMC's services. I mean, disrupting TSMC would mean disrupting the entire global tech ecosystem, an outcome even a tariff-heavy administration might hesitate to fully trigger. And while everyone is scared, the news of rising prices should silently lead to even higher margins for TSM (or at least to their longer-term stabilization).

TSM's Valuation Update

Right away, the market's consensus sees TSM's EPS for FY2025 at $9.07, which is 28.89% above last year's figure. The implied price-to-earnings of the firm (if it reports in line with the estimate) is ~16.71x. So the FY2025 PEG ratio is currently ~0.58x. Many investment banks - Bank of America analysts, for example, lowered their EPS estimates recently, but kept their price targets above the current stock price. BofA analysts, while lowering their price target slightly "due to tariff concerns and earthquake impacts", reiterated their "Buy" rating, saying the sell-off was "likely overdone" and noting that the stock's valuation "appeared attractive at 14x 2025E P/E compared to a trough of 10x during the 2022 downturn".

So I assume that the demand side is still strong enough to offset any concerning actions of TSM's partners (I mean, their potential decrease in orders). While TSMC has been beating the consensus figures for the past years since 2018, I'll assume the current EPS estimate for FY2025 is correct. So what's the fair P/E multiple for TSM, then? Well, given a likely multiple contraction, I think it's about 22x, given the historical norms and trends:

This gives me a fair value per share of ~$200, which is about 32% above the current stock price. This makes TSM a real bargain, even considering quite pessimistic inputs and no earnings beating in the next few quarters.

Risks And Your Takeaway

The main risk for TSM right now, like for almost any other tech stock, remains the geopolitical situation. A possible escalation of tensions between China and Taiwan or the actual introduction of highly disruptive US tariffs would drive down TSM stock price well below the current levels, no matter how strong its financials looked in the recent past. Also, the semiconductor industry is really inherently cyclical, we have to keep this in mind. Although the rise of AI seems to be creating strong secular growth tailwinds for TSM and its peers, I see an execution risk for TSMC's massive global expansion projects (Arizona, Japan, Germany) - in case the demand is satisfied and the pricing power of TSM is lost somehow, the current/prospective projects' ROI will unlikely to sufficient. If so, the valuation multiple might drop further.

Despite all these risk factors, I think that TSMC has a unique place in the semi tech sector. Its wide moat in a niche so crucial for the global AI usage expansion, it really can't be overestimated as of today, in my opinion. The recent developments, when we zoom out the tariffs fears and look at the demand/supply balance, strongly suggest that TSM's positioning is only getting stronger. So even if we use skeptical assumptions in our EPS and implied P/E ratio projections, we'll likely get a massive undervaluation. For what I took as inputs, my modeling says TSM stock is at least 32% undervalued today following the Q1 results (and after the dip in the stock price). So that's why I'm upgrading my rating to "Strong Buy" today.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: My Dividend Stock Portfolio: New All-Time Dividend Record In March - 100 Holdings With 15 Buys
data.description:
Portfolio Changes in March

In March 2025, U.S. markets took a hit as trade tensions flared up again and haven't stopped ever since. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq both dropped over 10% from recent highs, officially entering correction territory. The sell-off was sharp and triggered by a wave of new tariffs announced by President Trump in early April on imports from Canada, Mexico, and China, sparking fears of a renewed trade war.​

Tech stocks were hit hardest, while banks posted solid profits but warned that inflation and weaker consumer spending could be on the horizon if tariffs stay in place. Luxury brands had mixed results—some held up well, others not so much.

Markets ended the month on a cautious note, with eyes now on how trade talks unfold and what the Fed might do next. April kicked off with chaos after Trump’s “Liberation Day” tariffs sent markets into a tailspin. China hit back hard, the Dow plunged over 1,600 points, and protests erupted. Even with a partial pause, tariffs stayed at record highs, fueling recession fears.

My investment activity in March managed to reclaim the $1,000 threshold after last month's sharp drop. I channeled around $1,200 into the markets and plan to increase that further in April, given highly volatile markets with plenty of buying opportunities.

My main picks in March were Hercules Capital (HTGC), Blue Owl Capital (OBDC) and Ares Capital (ARCC).

In March, all my moves added $101. This is fractionally above my aim to lift my dividend income by about $100 each month, mostly doable thanks to the high yields from BDCs. I am hoping to increase that in the coming months to at least $120 and possibly $150.

Overall, the average yield on cost of my new investments in March averaged around 7.1%, reinforcing my belief in BDCs as a core part of my portfolio. I'm banking on their dividends staying steady - that's what I'm all about when it comes to generating income. Time will tell if I'm on the money or just overly optimistic.

All these purchases break down as follows:

All net purchases in March can be found below:

Dividend Income in March 2025

Dividend income hit a new all-time record of $1,404 in March, surpassing my expectations as I underestimated how much dividend income my purchases over the last twelve months actually contributed. This represents growth of 17% Y/Y and 21% sequentially. Adjusting for semi-annual dividend payments from Commonwealth Bank of Australia (OTCPK:CBAUF), Hercules Capital's odd dividend schedule and other smaller dividend payments returns Y/Y growth of 17% and 5% sequentially.

Beyond that, on an annual basis, the biggest income drivers are by far Ares Capital Corporation, a stock I am running at minimum on a bi-weekly savings plan (but most of the time I am buying stock at least once per week and sometimes daily) and where my contributions quickly translated into meaningful dividend income and the MidCap Financial Investment Corporation (MFIC). Dividends from Ares have already comfortably cleared the big psychological milestone of $200. This quarter's dividend amounts to $244 and will soon eclipse $250. Dividend income from MFIC is also rising quickly - although not on the same level as that from ARCC - and has now passed $125. Dividend income from Hercules Capital is on a roll and also cleared a psychological milestone of $150 coming in at almost $160 already.

Similarly striking is that net dividends from monthly dividend payers have again comfortably cleared the $100 level thanks to the big supplemental dividend from Main Street Capital.

In alignment with my investment strategy, I've incorporated a BDC/Non-BDC breakdown into my dividend income charts. This addition allows for more precise monitoring of this critical development.

Additionally, I have created a new visualization that dissects dividend income into two distinct categories: BDCs and Non-BDCs. These categories in turn are separated by dividend payment frequency into Monthly (blue) and Quarterly (orange) in absolute and relative terms.

For Q1 2025, BDCs accounted for 32% of my dividend income, up from 26% a year ago and down from the record 35% of Q4/2024.

This metric provides valuable insights and will help guide my investment decisions as it evolves over time.

In March, dividend income from my top three regular payers reached a staggering $445, a big jump from last year's average of $390 and getting closer to the major $500 level. I am buying MAIN on a monthly basis via savings plans and MidCap and Ares usually at least once a week. Based on that, I might be able to hit that $500 target in Q4 assuming at least stable dividends for the time being.

All this is depicted below:

Here is a chart that shows the development of my net dividend income by month over time between 2015 and 2025. You can see the growth of my dividend income and the average annual dividend for each year:

This chart is my favorite because it illustrates the progression of my dividend income over time and allows me to easily see the average annual dividend for each year.

Next, I have plotted all the individual dividend payments I have received, coloring them by year and arranging the years side by side instead of horizontally as in previous updates. This visualization allows for a more comprehensive analysis of the distribution of my dividend payments over time.

By visualizing the data in this manner, we get a clear picture of how dividend payments are spread out over different years, offering valuable insights into the overall growth of my dividend income over the years.

This visualization is initially quite cluttered, but it contains a wealth of information. It shows every individual dividend payment I have received since I began my investment journey in 2015, represented as a circle that is colored and sized according to its contribution. The view is organized by month and year, allowing for a more comprehensive analysis of the development of my dividend income over time. For each year and month, a white rectangle indicates the average monthly dividend. The area where dividends fall below the average is filled in dark red, while the area above the average is colored dark green.

This type of data visualization allows for a detailed examination of the distribution of my dividend payments over time and enables me to easily identify trends and patterns in my income growth.

Now, zooming in on March, we can immediately see a number of big green circles in a sea of black. The bigger the circle and the bigger the distance to the previous circle for the same stock, the bigger is the change in dividend income compared to the prior years.

The importance of Ares Capital Corporation is clear, with a reddish circle indicating the massive jump in dividend income over the last 12 months due to heavy ongoing monthly investments, and I have no plans to stop these monthly savings plans. Similarly, the big year-over-year increases in dividend income from MAIN, MFIC and GAIN also clearly stand out visually in that chart.

Apart from that, when it comes to the dividends for 2025, I notice mostly dark blue circles at the top of the scale, which is exactly what I want to see. These big circles represent rising dividend income, and my goal is to have as many of them as possible at the top of the chart.

Overall, my aim is to observe numerous large orange circles positioned at the highest point on the scale. This signifies a positive trend of increasing dividend income, which is precisely what I'm striving for.

Another way to express the monthly dividend income is in terms of Gifted Working Time (GWT). Assuming an average hourly rate of $37.34 for 2025, my GWT and annual net dividends have been as follows:

  • 2018: 121 hours GWT, $3,000 in annual net dividends
  • 2019: 142 hours GWT, $3,600 in annual net dividends
  • 2020: 152 hours GWT, $3,800 in annual net dividends
  • 2021: 180 hours GWT, $5,050 in annual net dividends
  • 2022: 229 hours GWT, $6,400 in annual net dividends
  • 2023: 272 hours GWT, $8,800 in annual net dividends
  • 2024: 284 hours GWT, $10,200 in annual net dividends
  • 2025: Targeting at least 295 hours GWT, $11,000 in annual net dividends

The overall target for the year with $11,000 in annual net dividends is very conservative, but I expect that I will have to take out some risk from my portfolio as I am eyeing to raise capital for real estate investments if I encounter the right deal and definitely for a lot of traveling. This would represent a growth rate of around 8% which admittedly is a low target but definitely something I am willing to update as the year unfolds. If things go differently, the dream target would be to pass the next milestone of $12,000 which would require around 18% dividend growth.

That would be a great achievement, but nothing I am banking on to reach this year - it would be the icing on the cake.

Across the years, my YTD Dividend Race has been as follows:

  • 2018: Disappointing
  • 2019: Phenomenal, benefiting from a low baseline in the prior year
  • 2020: Fairly disappointing
  • 2021: Phenomenal
  • 2022: Much better than expected, fueled by the very weak euro
  • 2023: Breathtaking
  • 2024: Simply great

I have updated the YTD Dividend Race chart to show the development of YTD dividend income on a daily basis, allowing us to see that most of my dividend income is generated mid-month and towards the end of the month. The chart also breaks down the development by month for the current year, making it easy to identify significant jumps in income.

Expressed in GWT, it presents itself as follows:

What this shows is as follows:

  1. All time (blue area) - Around 1,565 hours, or 196 days, of active work have been replaced with passive income since the start of my dividend journey. Assuming a five-day workweek, this translates into more than half a year in total.
  2. Full-year 2025 (green bars) - Around 73 hours, or 9.1 days, of active work have been replaced with passive income in 2025, which is basically equivalent to almost two full working weeks funded with dividends. That is great progress, and I can't wait to add another month to that statistic.
  3. Highlighted in pink is the accumulated total at the end of the current reporting month (March).

This visualization allows us to see the overall impact of my dividend income on the amount of active work I am able to replace with passive income. It also enables us to see the progress made year-to-date, as well as the accumulated total at the end of the current reporting month.

Upcoming April Dividends

Given the majority of my savings plans feature companies that pay dividends in January such as W. P. Carey (WPC), Altria (MO), Cisco Systems (CSCO), JPMorgan, Toronto-Dominion Bank, Bank of Nova Scotia, Stryker (SYK), Medtronic (MDT) as well as ongoing investments into stocks like Rithm Capital (RITM) and Annaly Capital (NLY), it surely is going to be a big month that should easily set a new all-time dividend record for the month of April.

Overall, I am expecting more than $700 in dividend income and can't wait to see how the numbers will stack up in around 3 weeks.
