data.title: Viant's Hidden Leverage: Why This Small-Cap Could Scale Fast
data.description:
Investment thesis

Viant Technology Inc. (NASDAQ:DSP) 's scalable, fixed-cost-heavy structure positioned it to convert modest revenue growth into outsized profitability. Viant returned to profitability in 2024, and its strong balance sheet adds to its financial flexibility. While peer comparisons show it lags in margins and scale, its improving cost efficiency and modest Reinvestment needs to suggest a meaningful upside if execution continues to improve. The investment case hinges on Viant’s ability to scale while maintaining cost discipline. Based on moderate valuation assumptions, there is more than a 30% margin of safety.

Business background

Viant provides a technology platform that powers data-driven, privacy-conscious digital advertising across the open web. At its core, Viant is an advertising software company whose flagship product is Adelphic, a demand-side platform (DSP) designed for marketers and advertising agencies to plan, buy, and measure digital advertising across various channels.

While Viant IPOed in 2021 as an advertising software company, it has since repositioned itself as a more technology-driven, cloud-based advertising platform. Nonetheless, its core DSP offering and services have remained consistent.

Viant currently generates all of its revenue from the United States.

Viant serves the digital advertising and marketing technology sector. This is a high-growth sector, as exemplified by the following:

“US digital advertising spending will surpass $300 billion for the first time in 2024… a 15.1% increase over 2023… Double-digit-percentage growth is expected to continue through 2026…Growth will moderate to 9.7% in 2027 and 9.2% in 2028 due to tougher comparatives.” EMarketer

“The marketing technology market in the United States is expected to reach a projected revenue of US$ 300,542.5 million by 2030. A compound annual growth rate of 19.5% is expected…from 2024 to 2030.” Grand View Research

These market trends suggest a robust and growing demand for its products and services in the U.S. It is not surprising that from 2021 to 2023, Viant's topline grew at 8.9% CAGR.

Profit drivers

Note that because of its pass-through tax structure, it makes more sense to focus on the operating profits. I will discuss this tax issue a bit more in the valuation section.

Post-IPO, Viant incurred operating loss from 2021 to 2023 but delivered an operating profit of USD 3.5 million in 2024.

The operating profit picture was because it was operating below its breakeven level from 2021 to 2023. Refer to the right part of Chart 1.

Post-IPO, Viant’s cost structure has been characterized by fixed costs accounting for slightly more than half of total costs (fixed plus variable). In such a setup, any increase in revenue tends to drive a disproportionately higher increase in operating profit.

Fixed cost margin in 2024 is lower than that in 2021. A big part of the fixed cost comes from the Selling, General, and Administration (SGA) cost. You can see from the left part of Chart 1 that the SGA margin has also declined significantly since 2022, while the gross profit margin has been relatively flat.

Notes to Chart 1:

a. SGA margin = Selling, Administration, and General expenses/Revenue.

b. I broke down the operating profits into fixed costs and variable costs.

  • Fixed cost = SGA, Depreciation & Amortization, and R&D.
  • Variable cost = Cost of Sales – Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Contribution = Revenue – Variable Cost.
  • Contribution margin = Contribution/Revenue.

The findings suggest that Viant is on a clear path toward sustained profitability. This is supported by a strong track record of revenue growth and a cost structure that favours operating leverage.

  • From 2018 to 2024, Viant’s revenue grew at 17.8 CAGR %. Despite a brief dip in 2022, the company rebounded sharply with a 31% year-over-year revenue increase in 2024, underscoring its resilience and the demand for its platform.
  • What makes this growth particularly powerful is Viant’s post-IPO cost structure, where incremental revenue would contribute significantly to operating profit.

Peer comparison

I compared Viant with some companies in the AdTech industry, focusing on those where revenue from the US is a big portion of the respective total revenue.

  • Magnite (MGNI) is the largest independent sell-side platform specializing in programmatic video and CTV ad monetization for publishers. The US accounted for ¾ of its, 2024 revenue.
  • MediaAlpha (MAX) operates a vertical-specific customer acquisition platform using programmatic bidding in sectors like insurance and travel. It will serve only the US in 2024.
  • Perion Network (PERI) is a diversified AdTech firm offering solutions across search, social, and display, anchored by a major partnership with Microsoft. In 2024, the US accounted for about ¾ of its revenue.
  • The Trade Desk (TTD) is a leading independent DSP enabling programmatic ad buying across digital channels, with strong dominance in connected TV serving the US.

As shown in Table 1, Viant had the smallest, 2024 revenue and one of the lowest growth rates. You can see that all the panel companies achieved double-digit growth rates, pointing to a strong growth sector.

Over the past 4 years, while the trajectory is improving, Viant’s financial quality still trails that of its listed peers. Refer to Charts 2 and 3.

  • Viant had one of the worst returns on capital over the past 4 years.
  • While Viant’s EBIT margin has improved since reaching the bottom in 2022, it is still the worst performer in 2024 as there were corresponding improvements by the panel companies.
  • It also had one of the worst Levered Free Cash Flow margins over the past 4 years.
  • I would rate Viant's EPS as average at best.

Given that the peers not only generate significantly larger revenue but also deliver stronger growth rates, the comparison suggests that scale matters in this industry. Larger players appear better positioned to leverage operating efficiencies, attract premium demand, and reinvest at higher returns - advantages that may explain their superior profitability and capital returns.

Financial Position

Viant’s financial position as of end-2024 reflects a strong balance sheet with a solid foundation characterized by liquidity strength, prudent leverage, and disciplined capital allocation.

  • As of the end of Dec 2024, almost half of its total assets were held as cash. This is equal to USD 205 million.
  • It had a low Debt Equity ratio of 10% at the end of Dec 2024.
  • From 2018 to 2024, there was only one year when it failed to deliver a positive cash flow from operations. During this period, it generated USD 150 million in cash flow from operations, compared to the cumulative loss of USD 78 million.
  • Post-IPO, it had an average of USD 5 million per year of Reinvestment. I defined Reinvestment = CAPEX & Acquisitions – Depreciation & Amortization + increase in Net Working Capital. This is low because a part of the CAPEX & Acquisition was offset by the Depreciation & Amortization. This is a good sign because as the company scales up, a big part of the profits could be returned to shareholders or used to reduce debt.
  • It had a reasonable capital allocation plan, as shown in Table 2. The cash flow from operations was more than sufficient to fund its acquisition and CAPEX, with the excess used to reduce debt and returned to shareholders.

Valuation

I adopted the following picture in valuing Viant:

  • The base revenue would be the 2024 revenue.
  • The contribution margin, capital efficiency, and Reinvestment rate would be sustained based on the post-IPO average.
  • Fixed cost would be based on the post-IPO average and would increase at 4% per annum.
  • Tax rate of 24%.

I considered two scenarios in valuing Viant, based on different revenue growth trajectories:

Scenario 1: Conservative Case

Assumes revenue grows at 9% in Year 1, tapering linearly to the long-term GDP growth rate of 4% by Year 6. This is anchored on Viant’s post-IPO CAGR of 9%, reflecting a more measured outlook.

Scenario 2: Moderate Case

Assumes revenue grows at 13% in Year 1, also tapering to 4% by Year 6. The 13% rate reflects the midpoint between the 9% post-IPO growth rate and the 18% CAGR recorded between 2018 and 2024, suggesting potential upside if the historical growth trend persists.

Based on these assumptions, the estimated intrinsic values are:

  • USD 11 per share under Scenario 1.
  • USD 25 per share under Scenario 2.

With the market price at USD 13 per share (as of 14 April 2025), only Scenario 2 offers a meaningful margin of safety.

Considering its revenue growth track record, I think that Scenario 2 is more realistic.

Valuation model

I valued Viant based on a multi-stage Free Cash Flow to the Firm (FCFF) model, as shown in Table 3.

The basic equations used in the model are:

FCFF = EBIT(1 – t) – Reinvestment.

EBIT = Revenue X Contribution margin – Fixed cost.

The contribution margin and fixed cost are based on the operating model shown in the right part of Chart 1.

Notes to Table 3:

a. Proportionate reduction

b. Pegged to growth rate

c. Assumed no improvement

d. Assumed growth at terminal rate

e. Revenue X Margin and after accounting for Fixed costs

f. Assumed no improvement

g. b X f

h. FCFF for each year = e - g

j. Refer to the WACC table

k. NPV for each year = (h X j)

l. Terminal for the year discounted at terminal growth rate

m. 5 years NPV + terminal value

n. Inclusive of any excess TCE. Non-operating assets, MI and Debt

o. Based on the number of shares

The WACC used in the valuation model was based on the first page results of a Google search for “Viant Technology WACC”. Refer to Table 4.

Tax issues

Viant operates through Viant Technology LLC, which is treated as a pass-through entity for U.S. federal income tax purposes. Under this structure, Viant is taxed only on its share of the LLC’s income, while the remaining income is allocated to other members, who are taxed individually.

This arrangement contributes to Viant’s low effective tax rate:

  • Viant holds only a partial economic interest in Viant Technology LLC.
  • The consolidated financial statements reflect the full operating results of the LLC, but only the income attributable to Viant is subject to corporate tax.
  • The remainder of the income, allocated to non-controlling/minority interests, is not taxed at the corporate level.

This structural feature is explicitly discussed in Viant’s 2024 Form 10-K, which notes that its tax provision is based solely on its allocable share of LLC income.

However, this tax structure - and the resulting low effective tax rate - is not directly applicable to my valuation.

In my model, I first derive the value of the firm based on operating income, and then subtract debt and minority interests to arrive at the equity value. This approach effectively accounts for the pass-through structure, justifying the use of a standard nominal tax rate of 24%, rather than the reported 2024 effective tax rate of just 2%.

Risks and limitations

The key valuation driver for Viant is its future revenue growth rate. For Scenario 2, I have assumed a 13% growth in the next year, gradually tapering to the long-term GDP growth rate by Year 6.

This assumption appears realistic given the context. While Viant’s post-IPO growth averaged 9% per year compounded, its CAGR from 2018 to 2024 was nearly double that. Additionally, sector-wide trends suggest ongoing tailwinds in digital advertising and marketing technology adoption.

Importantly, Viant’s current cost structure and low Reinvestment needs translated into a significantly improved projected free cash flow to the firm (FCFF) relative to its past performance. Refer to the right part of Chart 4. If the revenue growth assumption proves cautious, there is a meaningful upside to the valuation.

However, as the left part of Chart 4 illustrates, future free cash flow is also sensitive to operating margins and cost discipline.

A key downside risk lies in whether Viant can maintain its margins and cost efficiency as it scales. While there is reason for optimism, this remains a critical execution challenge:

  • Peer comparisons show that Viant is not yet a margin leader, which leaves it vulnerable to falling behind if competitors widen their operating advantage.
  • Since the IPO, Viant has reduced its fixed cost margin, which has helped restore profitability. Sustaining this discipline as it grows will be vital to unlocking operating leverage.

In short, the risk is not structural but operational. The company’s ability to scale without margin erosion will ultimately determine whether the profit trajectory - and valuation upside - materializes as projected.

Conclusion

Viant operates in a fast-growing sector with tailwinds from the shift to data-driven, privacy-compliant digital advertising. Its post-IPO cost structure and low Reinvestment needs to provide strong operating leverage. These were traits that helped swing it back into profitability in 2024.

However, the company remains at an early stage in its margin recovery and scale-up journey. Peer comparisons show that Viant still lags in EBIT margin, capital returns, and free cash flow efficiency. It is also the smallest among its selected peers, which suggests that scale plays a role in sustaining higher growth and profitability in the AdTech space.

Despite this, Viant’s financial position is sound, and the business has shown resilience in rebounding from a dip in 2022. If management can continue to execute cost discipline while maintaining even modest topline growth, the resulting free cash flow trajectory under Scenario 2 could be materially better than its historical performance.

My valuation assumptions for the margin and cost efficiency imply a limited downside - yet a meaningful upside exists if Viant can unlock margin improvements or benefit from stronger-than-expected revenue growth.

Ultimately, the investment case for Viant hinges on operational execution. The company has the structure and sector positioning to succeed. Whether it does depends on its ability to scale without losing cost control.

data.title: Pair Trade 2.0: Buy Oil (USO), Sell Oil Stocks (XLE)
data.description:
Introduction

In late 2022, I shared a high-conviction pair trade idea on oil [long (NYSEARCA:USO)] and oil stocks [short (NYSEARCA:XLE)], citing a rare divergence:

Since peaking out in mid-2022, oil prices have been declining throughout the second half of this year. When this decline in oil started, oil stocks moved lower in tandem until mid-October 2022. The historical relationship between oil and oil stocks was holding up quite well. However, In the last month or so, oil has continued lower, but oil stocks have rallied higher. As you can see above, XLE is now sitting at record highs, whilst the oil price is sitting way below its recent peak.

As of now, there is a massive divergence between oil and oil stocks. And historical data shows that such a divergence is a rare anomaly. Sooner or later, this divergence will be resolved in one of three ways -

1. Oil prices go up, and Oil stocks go down

2. Oil prices stay flat, and Oil stocks go down

3. Oil prices go down, and Oil stocks go down even more

In my view, the third scenario is the most likely one to materialize.

Source: Pair Trade: Buy Oil (USO), Short Oil Stocks (XLE)

Now, over the past couple of years or so, USO has outperformed XLE by considerable margin for extended periods - rendering our suggested pair trade a prescient idea.

And, as it relates to the divergence, the gap between oil and oil stocks narrowed drastically on multiple occasions during this period:

Interestingly, this gap has expanded again in recent weeks, with oil price tumbling lower on trade war fears. While oil stocks have also suffered a drawdown in 2025 [XLE sitting just above 52-week lows], they may have more downside room if oil prices fail to rebound from their current malaise.

Why Is Oil Tanking?

As of 16th April 2025, oil prices have fallen precipitously since early April, reaching levels not seen since 2021 - WTI crude futures have traded below $60 per barrel and are currently around the low $60s. Brent Crude, the international benchmark, is also significantly down, trading in the mid-$60s per barrel.

Now, as you may know, oil prices are influenced by a complex interplay of geopolitical events, economic factors, supply and demand dynamics, and market sentiment. With the Trump administration's tariff policies threatening global trade, fears of an economic downturn have risen sharply. Consequently, the demand outlook for oil is weakening. Furthermore, OPEC+ recently announced an acceleration of oil production in May 2025 - adding an element of supply shock to the equation.

1. Trade Policy and Recession Fears:

  • US Tariffs: The recent imposition of significant tariffs by the United States on various countries, particularly China, has created concerns about a potential trade war. These tariffs are widely seen as a "large tax hike on American consumers" and have rattled global markets.
  • Demand Destruction: The fear is that these tariffs will weaken global economic activity, leading to a decrease in the demand for oil. As major economies slow down, there will be less need for transportation, manufacturing, and other oil-consuming activities.
  • Global Recession Concerns: The trade tensions are exacerbating existing fears of a broader global economic slowdown or even a recession. And as we have discussed in the past, weaker economic growth invariably translates to lower oil demand.

2. Increased Oil Supply:

  • OPEC+ Production Increase: On 3rd April 2025, The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) and its allies (OPEC+) surprisingly announced an acceleration of their planned production increases. This decision to boost supply by 411K barrels per day (vs est. 135K bpd), despite concerns about weakening demand, is adding downward pressure on oil prices. Now, this increase in OPEC+ production came after months of pressure from the US to increase oil supply and lower energy prices. While the Trump administration deems lower oil prices as a positive driver for economic growth and a benefit for consumers, the timing of this supply boost, coinciding with tariff-induced demand concerns, is problematic for the oil market.
  • US Production Levels: Now, even before the tariff announcements were made, there were concerns within the US shale oil industry about oversupply, with domestic oil production running at all-time highs and the new administration still calling for "drill baby drill".

In summary, the ongoing decline in oil prices is primarily driven by concerns about reduced global oil demand due to the negative economic impact of US tariffs and the unexpected increase in oil supply from OPEC+. As I see it, this combination of factors has created a bearish sentiment in the market, leading to a sharp downturn in oil prices.

Impact On The Energy Sector

Looking forward, The US Energy Information Administration (EIA) has recently revised its oil price forecasts downwards for 2025 and 2026, anticipating increased global oil inventories due to higher production and slower demand growth. They now expect Brent crude to average around $68 per barrel in 2025 and $61 per barrel in 2026.

Furthermore, earlier this week, OPEC lowered its oil demand outlook by 150K bpd for 2025 and 2026 due to changing trade dynamics -

The global economy showed a steady growth trend at the beginning of the year. However, recent trade related dynamics have introduced higher uncertainty to the short-term global economic growth outlook.

- OPEC Monthly Market Report

And, as per Goldman Sachs, in a bearish scenario, brent crude prices could fall under $40 per share by the end of 2025. Scary!

Now, the drop in oil prices and weakening consensus outlooks are naturally causing significant unease in the US shale oil industry, where production costs are generally higher. The current price levels are already falling below what many US producers need to profitably drill new wells, and so, if prices remain below $60-$65 per barrel for a prolonged period, there could be a slowdown in drilling and investment, accompanied by a significant contraction in oil industry earnings.

From a valuation standpoint, energy stocks are trading at undemanding Price-to-earnings multiples:

However, with earnings outlook in flux, equity markets have reacted negatively to the oil price decline, with significant losses seen in the stock prices of oilfield service companies and major oil producers.

Recession Or No Recession - The Divergence Will Resolve

For over two decades, the prices of oil and oil stocks have moved in tandem with each other. While the rare divergence we noted in late 2022 hasn't closed out thus far, the gap is now narrower and still very likely to get filled out sooner than later.

In the event of a recession, consumers and businesses cut back on spending, which leads to a drop in demand for oil. As demand drops, so do oil prices and oil stocks. With recession odds on the rise, both oil and oil stocks have pulled back in recent weeks. Now, if the trade war results in a global recession, both oil and oil stocks could tank further, with the latter catching down to the former.

On the other hand, even if the economy stays resilient, the divergence gap between oil and oil stocks will close out eventually. Why? If oil prices are low, producers won't invest in capacity expansion or slow production, curtailing the supply of oil. When the demand-supply equilibrium tilts in favor of demand, oil prices go up, and any divergence between oil prices and oil stocks closes out.

Final Thoughts: The Pair Trade Is On

While the current divergence gap between oil and oil stocks is not as large as it was back in late 2022, the pair trade idea of buying USO and selling XLE is still a worthy bet in this highly uncertain environment. Over the next 12–18 months, I think this pair trade could generate gross returns of 10-15%, regardless of the economy.

Key Takeaway: Buy oil (USO) and short oil stocks (XLE)

As always, thank you for reading, and happy investing. Please feel free to share any questions, concerns, or thoughts in the comments section below.
